policy_statement: Reduce non-CO2 GHGs from industrial processes.
mechanism_description: The policy aims to reduce non-CO2 greenhouse gases from industrial processes, lowering industrial process-related emissions.
primary_causal_pathway: Industrial processes → ↓ non-CO₂ GHG releases (CH₄/N₂O/F-gases) → ↓ CO₂e emissions → ↓ atmospheric forcing
causal_mechanism_detail:
(1) Change: Industrial facilities modify production processes and/or install controls to prevent, capture, destroy, or substitute non-CO₂ greenhouse gases generated during manufacturing (e.g., chemical production, metals, semiconductor/electronics, refrigerant-related industrial uses).  
(2) Mediating process: Reduced formation of these gases, improved containment, leak prevention, capture and destruction (e.g., oxidation/abatement), or substitution with lower-GWP alternatives lowers direct process emissions.  
(3) Outcome: Lower facility- and sector-level non-CO₂ process emissions (lower CO₂e), reducing net greenhouse effect and contributing to climate change mitigation.
dominant_pathway_test: The single biggest climate-relevant effect is the direct reduction of high-global-warming-potential non-CO₂ greenhouse gas emissions from industrial processes.
mechanism_classification_reasoning:
1) Dominant causal pathway: Industrial process changes/controls lead to lower non-CO₂ GHG emissions, yielding a direct reduction in CO₂e and atmospheric forcing.  
2) Why this primary over alternatives: While some measures may also improve operational efficiency or reduce local pollution, the defining and largest climate effect across this mechanism is direct GHG abatement (mitigation), not energy/resource savings (resource efficiency) or risk reduction (adaptation) or ecosystem carbon (NBS).  
3) Edge cases applied: If a policy reduces non-CO₂ gases indirectly via efficiency upgrades, the mitigation effect still comes through reduced GHG emissions; classification remains mitigation because the mechanism is explicitly “non-CO₂ abatement.”  
4) Applicability to ALL instances: Any policy that targets non-CO₂ emissions from industrial processes shares the same primary outcome—reduced process GHG emissions—so “Mitigation” holds consistently.  
5) Note on subtypes: Any secondary subtype (e.g., methane vs F-gases; point-source vs fugitive; control tech vs substitution) must be assigned at the individual policy level based on specific text and evidence.
mechanism_edge_case_notes: Some “non-CO₂ industrial” actions can overlap with air-pollution control or energy-efficiency programs, but when the policy intent/mechanism is explicitly to abate non-CO₂ GHGs from processes, the dominant climate pathway remains direct mitigation via CO₂e reductions.
instance_edge_case_notes: No instrument is specified (could be implemented via standards, LDAR, bans/phase-downs, incentives, or pricing), so classification defaults to a target/goal statement rather than a concrete regulatory or spending instrument.
